coredata.dc:title: Improved performance with boron-doped carbon quantum dots in perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Çisem
Yılmaz, Tuğbahan
Toprak, Ayşegül
Büyükbekar, Alihan
Kuş, Mahmut
Ersöz, Mustafa
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the using of boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs), which can convert ultraviolet to blue light, as an additive in MAPbI3 based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) for improved photovoltaic performance. After a successful facile synthesis of B-CQDs, our work showed that the appropriate addition of B-CQDs (5% vol.) into MAPbI3 absorber layer can passivate the grain boundaries of perovskite crystals, improve the crystallinity and the grain sizes. Additionally, optical, and photovoltaic characteristics showed that the addition of B-CQDs can efficiently improve the charge transport properties and the hole extraction, while decreasing the recombination losses. In comparison to control device, power conversion efficiency (PCE) of champion device was increased from 10.4% to 12.7%. This approach of constructing inverted p-i-n PSCs with B-CQDs additives offers a simple way for the preparation of efficient and stable perovskite photovoltaics for future application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B-CQDs
Perovskite solar cells
Passivation
Additive engineering
Film coverage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of synthesis of the B-CQDs.
Flow diagram perovskite solar cell process.
AFM images of MAPbI3 thin films prepared with/without B-CQDs.
XRD patterns of MAPbI3 thin films prepared with/without B-CQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of MAPbI3 thin films prepared with/without B-CQDs.
Tauc plots calculated from the UV-Vis absorption data of MAPbI3 thin films prepared with/without B-CQDs.
(a) J-V curves of the best-performing devices and (b) their EQE spectra.
Box charts of the photovoltaic parameters of MAPbI3-0% B-CQDs and MAPbI3-5% B-CQDs PSCs fabricated.
The average roughness (Ra), grain size distribution, crystal size, and optical band gap values perovskite films prepared with/without B-CQDs.
Device parameters of the perovskite solar cells prepared with/without B-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Improved performance with boron-doped carbon quantum dots in perovskite solar cells

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Validation.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The Member of Turkish Academy of Sciences (TUBA)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the using of boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs), which can convert ultraviolet to blue light, as an additive in MAPbI3 based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) for improved photovoltaic performance. After a successful facile synthesis of B-CQDs, our work showed that the appropriate addition of B-CQDs (5% vol.) into MAPbI3 absorber layer can passivate the grain boundaries of perovskite crystals, improve the crystallinity and the grain sizes. Additionally, optical, and photovoltaic characteristics showed that the addition of B-CQDs can efficiently improve the charge transport properties and the hole extraction, while decreasing the recombination losses. In comparison to control device, power conversion efficiency (PCE) of champion device was increased from 10.4% to 12.7%. This approach of constructing inverted p-i-n PSCs with B-CQDs additives offers a simple way for the preparation of efficient and stable perovskite photovoltaics for future application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, organic-inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are the most promising alternative technology to silicon-based solar cells, which dominates the photovoltaic market. Thanks to great enhancement in photovoltaic performance (certified power conversion efficiency (PCE) reaching from 3.9% to over 25% [1]), low-cost and easy processability, studies performed on PSCs are increasing day by day. However, there are still some limitations for the commercialization of PSCs, including low stability under ambient conditions, moisture sensitivity, charge carrier recombination and lack of easy large-scale fabrication process [2]. To overcome these obstacles, many different approaches have been tried e.g., interfacial modification, device structure engineering, additive engineering, passivation, developing of non-spin-coating techniques and encapsulation methods [3–7]. Among all, the passivation appears to be one of the most efficient techniques for the preparation of high efficiency, low cost, and highly stable PSCs.

Passivation plays an important role to reduce the defects, which brings increase in the open circuit voltage (VOC) of device optimizing charge transfer properties at interfaces [8]. For PSCs, passivation also refers to physical passivation of grain boundaries of perovskite crystals, which improves the stability of devices under atmospheric moisture [9]. Passivation can be engineered via different techniques such as interface passivation by using of self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) [10], self-passivation by PbI2 or CH3NH3I (MAI) [11], grain boundary passivation by addition of organic molecules or nanomaterials [12,13], and, interestingly, surface passivation by oxygen [14]. Among all, the grain boundary passivation is an effective way to improve the crystallinity as well as the grain sizes, thereby, the film quality perovskite film. So far, several grain boundary passivation materials have been reported to interact with the perovskite charged defects. For example, organic Lewis bases thiophene and pyridine have been reported as passivation molecules for the interaction of defect states of perovskite, thereby improve the photoluminescence and device performance by Noel et al. [15]. There have been various studies on the using of polymer additives as passivation strategy. In a study, a Lewis type organic polymer passivation molecule, PMMA, has been used to incorporate into formamidinium tin tri-iodide based perovskite grain boundaries due to its large size advantage [16]. Polymer passivation molecules has been reported to be used as perovskite grain boundary cross-linkers, which leads to obtain stable interactions and enhanced film stability [17]. Inorganic additives are the other most common-used passivation molecules, which act like nucleation sites and lead to form large grain growth [18]. In addition to these materials, recent reports showed that the incorporation of quantum dots (QDs) into perovskite absorber layer helps to passivate it and prevent charge carrier recombination [19,20]. QDs are semiconductor materials that can absorb incoming radiation and re-emit it with the tunable spectrum [21]. Among them, the tunable size, optical and electrical properties of carbon QDs (CQDs) make them more attracted to be used in optoelectronic devices. In recent years, various studies about the applications of CQDs in light-emitting diodes, sensitizers, solar cells, and bionanocomposites have been reported [22,23]. Even though there have been researches on the addition of carbon nanomaterials to enhance the performance of perovskite-based optoelectronic devices, researchers uncommonly investigated the effect of the presence of functional groups on CQDs passivation materials on the perovskite formation. By introducing hetero-atoms into CQDs, their intrinsic properties, such as electronic, fluorescence, or catalytic properties can be modulated. For example, nitrogen has been reported as a typical dopant for CQDs to improve photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs [24]. Furthermore, there has been interesting work using carbon quantum dots doped in perovskite solar cells [25–30]. For instance, Hadadian et al., used the nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-RGO) into perovskite films for improving the photovoltaic performance of PSCs [28]. By doping the N-RGO to the perovskite film, perovskite grain size increased, decreasing grain boundaries and increasing PCE to 18.7%. It could be said that the N-RGO contributed to improving the photovoltaic parameters. Gan et al. reported the Graphite-N doped graphene quantum dots (GN-GQDs) in perovskite layer for obtaining high photovoltaic parameters of perovskite solar cells [29]. After doping with GN-GQDs, the charge transport increased and the charge recombination decreased in PSCs, thus achieved high PCE of 19.89%. Wen et al. introduced the carbon quantum dots as additive in perovskite layer to enhance photovoltaic performance of perovskite solar cell [30]. In this study, they reached that the average power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CQD doped perovskite solar cell was increased from 15.8% to 18.8%.

In our previous report, we examined the incorporation of nitrogen by doping as CQDs (N-CQDs) into methyl ammonium lead iodide perovskite (CH3NH3PbI3-MAPbI3) absorber layer, and the role of nitrogen in device performance were identified [31]. In the presented study, the role of boron doped CQDs (B-CQDs) was aimed and the CQDs (B-CQDs) was introduced into perovskite absorber layer to investigate the enhancement in photovoltaic performance and reproducibility results. Previous studies presented that the presence of B atoms in carbon structure promotes the hole extraction and leads to better charge collection [32,33]. The addition of B-CQDs promotes the forming of continuous perovskite film with reduced defects and larger grain size. Consequently, the best performing solar cell increased from initial PCE of 10.4% to PCE of 12.7%, thanks to improved optical properties, band structure, better charge collection, and highly oriented MAPbI3 morphology. Impressively, high reproducibility results were observed for the PSCs prepared with the addition of B-CQDs.

The schematic diagram of synthesis of the B-CQDs is depicted in Fig. 1. 4 g of boric acid (Merck), 4 g of citric acid (VWR, 99.5%), 4 g of urea (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) and 80 mL of deionized (DI) water were placed into a teflon-coated cylinder stainless steel autoclave. Then, the prepared solution was heated hydrothermally at 120 °C for 12 h using an oven. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the solution was removed from the solvent by centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The remaining were dried by rotary evaporation followed by drying in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h. Finally, B-CQDs were dissolved in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) at concentration of 10 mg/mL to prepare the additive solution.

The structure of PSCs in an inverted (p-i-n) architecture is presented in Fig. 2. Firstly, indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass substrates were ultrasonically cleaned by Hellmanex (Sigma Aldrich, Hellmanex III, cleaner for glass and quartz substrates), DI water, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for 10 min each, followed by drying with nitrogen gun. Thereafter, the cleaned substrates were treated by oxygen plasma (Diener Plasma System Femto PCCE - Plasma Cleaner) for 5 min to eliminate organic residual. PEDOT:PSS solution was prepared (Clevios, PVP Al 4083) by doping of 0.5 vol% Zonyl FS300 and 5.0 vol% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma Aldrich, 99.9%). All the following spin-coating was performed in ambient atmosphere. A 40 nm PEDOT:PSS layer was coated by spin-coating of prepared PEDOT:PSS solution at 4000 rpm for 60 s and annealed at 120 °C for 10 min. The perovskite solution was prepared in 2.5 mL γ-butyrolactone (GBL) by dissolving of PbI2 (1.23 M) and CH3NH3I (1.54 M) and was stirred at 70 °C for 2 h. For the preparation of B-CQDs added perovskite solution, 0%, 1%, 3% and 5% vol. of B-CQDs solution were added into MAPbI3 solution, followed by stirring for overnight. Then prepared perovskite solution with B-CQDs were spin coated at 6000 rpm for 50 s by pouring of 70 μL toluene at last 35 s leading to form a uniform perovskite film. The perovskite coated substrates were annealed at 110 °C for 30 min. After cooling the coated substrates down, electron transport layer solution prepared by dissolving of 40 mg PCBM (Lumtec) in 1 mL chlorobenzene (CB): chloroform (CF) (1:1) was spin coated onto perovskite layer for 60 s at 2000 rpm and annealed for 30 min at 110 °C. Subsequently, the PSCs fabrication was finished after Al (60 nm) electrodes were prepared by physical vapour deposition at 1 × 10−6 mbar.

The UV–vis absorption spectra was recorded on a Biochrom Libra S22 to obtain the optical properties of B-CQDs and thin films. Photoluminescent (PL) spectra of the B-CQD solution was measured on a Perkin Elmer LS-55 photoluminescent spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) testing of B-CQDs were conducted on a JEOL JEM 2100 FHRTEM. To confirm the presence of boron element on B-CQDs, Scanning Electron Microscopy and Energy Dispersive X-Ray analyses were performed by a Zeiss EVO LS 10. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images of perovskite thin films were taken by NT-MDT AFM NTEGRA Solaris in “tapping” mode. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker Advance D8 X-ray diffractometer. The current density-voltage (J–V) curves of PSCs fabricated with/without the addition of B-CQDs were recorded on an ATLAS solar simulator using as AM 1.5 light source with xenon arc lamp and a Keithley 2400 source meter at room temperature under inert atmosphere. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) measurements of best performing devices were performed on a Newport (QE-PV-SI) with a 300 W xenon light source as a function of wavelength.

The optical properties of B-CQDs were studied by ultraviolet-visible (UV) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and the results were shown in Fig. S1(a) and Fig. S1(b), respectively. The absorption peak of B-CQDs centered at around 330 nm which is attributed to n–π * absorption band [34]. Since PL behavior is completely dependent on the excitation wavelength, the PL spectrum of B-CQDs was acquired at excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 360 nm, and a strong emission peak centered at around 430 nm, as shown in Fig. S1(b). As excitation wavelength increased, the intensity of emission peak of B-CQDs increased at excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 350 nm, then decreased at excitation wavelength 360 nm. As seen, the brightest PL peak was observed at excitation wavelengths 350 nm correspond to bright blue-light under UV-light, given in the inset to Fig. S1(a). In addition, the PL quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be about 12.4% with Coumarin 2 as a reference, which reported our previous work [35]. The result of optical properties of B-CQDs suggest that the blue-fluorescent B-CQDs should have a higher highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital energy gap [36].

Structural characterization is crucial for B-CQDs synthesized. Thus, SEM-EDX mapping technique was performed to figure out the presence of boron element in B-CQDs. According to the result of SEM-EDX mapping as shown in Fig. S2(a-f), three main elements were measured, consisting of 7.77% boron (B), 33.00% carbon (C) and 59.23% oxygen (O). As seen, the main component is carbon, whereas the presence of oxygen correlated with functional groups like hydroxyl in B-CQDs. The result of SEM-EDX mapping indicates that the CQDs were successfully doped with boron atoms. Additionally, the particle size was analyzed by TEM technique. TEM images of B-CQDs were shown in Fig. S3. B-CQDs depicted uniformly distributed spherical form, and the average diameter of B-CQDs was measured around 10 nm. Provided that zoomed in to the TEM image, the B-CQDs diameter appears to be less than 6 nm. This result is correlated with our previous studies [35,37]. Both SEM-EDX and TEM results confirm that B-CQDs were successfully prepared.

To investigate the effect of the presence of B-CQDs on morphological properties of thin films, the morphology of perovskite films was examined by the AFM in tapping mode by a cantilever coated with Pt/Ir while applying a 5 V bias under ambient conditions. Furthermore, grain size distribution and the average surface roughness (Ra) were analyzed. Fig. 3 represents two dimensional (2D) of the height images of 10 µm × 10 µm scan area of perovskite films. The Ra values are measured to be 13.076 nm for 0 vol% of B-CQDs, 15.931 nm for 1 vol% of B-CQDs, 16.462 nm for 3 vol% of B-CQDs and 21.046 nm for 5 vol% of B-CQDs which are summarized in Table 1. It is seen that the Ra values of the perovskite films increased with the increasing vol% of B-CQDs addition. The perovskite film with 5 vol% of B-CQDs showed the highest Ra than those of other surfaces. According to similar reports, having a rough surface could be attributed to the bigger perovskite crystal size and this leads to better light absorption and scattering [23,38].

The addition of CQDs could promote the crystallization process of perovskite. As a result, the perovskite grain size could change related to PbI2 and CQDs interaction. The using of B-CQDs as an additive could result a strong interaction between PbI2 and the CO increasing the perovskite grain size to delay rapid perovskite crystallization. To support this hypothesis, the grain sizes of perovskite thin films prepared with and without B-CQDs were measured by a software integrated with AFM and the results were summarized in Table 1. It increased from 75.07 nm for perovskite film with 0% vol. of B-CQDs to 80.97 nm, 92.45 nm, and 116.24 nm for perovskite film with 1, 3, and 5 vol% of B-CQDs, respectively. Also, it was reported that the increasing grain size distribution of perovskite thin film can lead to a decrease in grain boundaries and carrier recombination of the perovskite films while stimulating charge transport [39]. Thus, PSC with high photovoltaic performance can be achieved.

XRD characterization of perovskite thin films were performed to determine the effect of B-CQDs on the structural properties. The XRD patterns of the surfaces were recorded between 10°− 70° as shown in Fig. 4. The XRD patterns of all surfaces consist of ten main peaks at around 14.4°, 20.3°, 23.7°, 24.8°, 28.7°, 32.2°, 40.9°, 43.4°, 50.6°, and 60.4° related to the (110), (200), (211), (202), (220), (310), (224), (314), (404) and (444) lattice planes for MAPbI3, respectively. These lattice planes are related to the orthorhombic phase of MAPbI3 [40]. In addition, the presence of non-stochiometric PbI2 phases can be observed at around 21.5°, 30.4°, and 35.4° diffraction positions [41]. As seen, the intensity of these PbI2 phase peaks slightly decreased as B-CQDs addition increased. Based on reported studies, PbI2 is a by-product of the degradation of MAPbI3 perovskite [42]. As desired, the increased peak intensities of the (110) and (220) lattice planes indicate that the addition of B-CQDs improves the crystal structure of the perovskite [43]. It can be concluded that the addition of B-CQDs showed an important effect on the crystal structure. In comparison to 1% vol. of B-CQDs, the peak intensities of the (110) and (220) lattice planes increased for 3% and 5% vol. of B-CQDs. This may comply with the blue-shift observed at the excitonic absorption peak [44]. To calculate the crystal size of perovskite films, the Scherrer Formula was used by the full width at half maximum (FWHM). The values of FWHM of (110) lattice planes were measured as 0.121, 0.112, 0.103, and 0.093 for 0, 1, 3, and 5 vol% of B-CQDs, respectively. Afterwards, the crystal sizes of MAPbl3 were calculated to be 69.17 nm for 0% vol. of B-CQDs, 74.73 nm for 1 vol% of B-CQDs, 81.26 nm for 3 vol% of B-CQDs, and 89.99 nm for 5 vol% of B-CQDs as summarized in Table 1. The MAPbl3 films prepared with B-CQDs additive presented improved perovskite crystallinity and increased grain size [43]. It can be said that B-CQDs can be used as an additive to improve the structure of perovskite films. The XRD results are in a harmony with the morphological characterization result.

To provide better understanding of the photophysical characteristics of the perovskite thin films, UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded and given in Fig. 5. As seen, the UV-Vis spectra of the perovskite films containing different amount of B-CQDs showed similar absorbance behavior. All perovskite films showed strong absorption of about 750 nm, indicating the optical band gap (Eg) between 1.5 eV and 1.6 eV. Additionally, the absorbance region in the range of 350–500 nm could be attributed to PbI2 [45]. Increasing B-CQDs additive in the perovskite films led to an increase in the UV–Vis absorption spectra at around 750 nm while decreasing in the range of 350–500 nm, which is beneficial for the stability of PSCs fabricated. As a result, it is clear that B-CQDs showed a strong absorption in the visible region. In addition, the Eg values of the perovskite films prepared with B-CQDs were calculated by Tauc equation [46] and the results were given in Fig. 6 and Table 1. The Eg values increased from 1.582 eV for 0% vol. of B-CQDs to 1.583 eV, 1.589 eV and 1.606 eV for 1, 3, and 5 vol% of B-CQDs, respectively. The calculated Eg presented a blue-shift in absorption region. It can be considered that it is suitable for solar cell applications due to its a good light-absorber.

To assess the influence of B-CQDs grain boundary passivation on photovoltaic characteristics, PSCs were fabricated in an inverted (p-i-n) architecture. Optimizing the concentration of additive in perovskite layer is crucial for highly efficient device fabrication. The J-V curves of different concentration of B-CQDs (0%, 1%, 3%, and 5% vol.) were given in Fig. 7(a) at forward scan direction. The photovoltaic performance results of best devices were summarized in Table 2. The addition of 0% vol. of B-CQDs presented open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.956 V, a short circuit current density (Jsc) of 15.5 mA cm−2 and a fill factor (FF) of 56.4% and a relatively low PCE of 10.45%. As seen, the addition of 5% vol. of B-CQDs into MAPbI3 resulted the best efficiency (12.73% of PCE) and this efficiency was obtained from Voc of 0.917 V, Jsc of 15.9 mA cm−2 and FF of 70.0%. According to the studies reported, the addition of higher vol. of CQDs could result in non-uniform coating of perovskite layer due to the strong aggregation of additive [47]. Therefore, the perovskite layer was optimized in a low concentration of B-CQDs. It should be noted that the vol. of B-CQDs increased to 5%, a significant improvement for PCE can be observed. This improvement was mainly tied to the high increase in FF, which was promoted by the optimal formation and better crystallinity of perovskite layer. A lower vol. of B-CQDs addition showed slight impact on the perovskite crystal structure. However, the presence of B-CQDs can show passivation effect and decrease the non-radiative recombination and trap-state density. An optimal vol. of B-CQDs (5% vol.) can successfully improve the FF due to the better interfacial contact between perovskite and bottom/upper layers [48]. The Jsc values slightly increased after the B-CQDs addition. This improvement was confirmed by EQE spectra of PSCs in Fig. 7(b). The devices prepared with the addition of 1% and 5% vol. of B-CQDs gave the highest EQE response in 400–600 nm in comparison to that of device prepared without B-CQDs. Additionally, the better EQE performance of PSCs prepared with B-CQDs between 350 and 800 nm related to the higher charge separation efficiency [49]. Consequently, the increased Jsc values agree with the findings in EQE curves. Furthermore, B-CQDs prepared in our study converted the light to blue light. As a result, MAPbI3 films prepared with B-CQDs addition showed an increase in EQE performance at shorter wavelengths.

The significant enhancement in FF values by the addition of B-CQDs could be attributed to decreased defect states, suppression in non-radiative recombination and the adjustment of energy band gap values [50]. Recent studies proposed that the boron atoms doping to the carbon structure could increase the hole extraction, which leads to efficient mobility and to reduce the charge back recombination at the thin film interfaces [51]. Also, CQDs have different functional groups like carbonyl, which passivate trap states and coordinate with the non-stoichiometric Pb ions in perovskite layer leading to form better film coverage [52]. The series and shunt resistance (Rs and Rsh, respectively) were extracted from J-V curves, and the results were listed in Table 2. It was revealed that the notable increase in the Rsh values was observed after the addition of B-CQDs, while the Rs values was decreased. These results are in a good matching with the increase in FF values. According to these device parameters, it could be said that the optimizing B-CQDs addition into perovskite active layer could efficiently improve the charge transport properties and the hole extraction, while decreasing the recombination losses. As well known, additives can show coordination capability and impact the perovskite crystallization. Additive types, concentration and coordination capability can affect the nucleation and grain growth mechanism of perovskite. B-CQDs coordinated with the non-stoichiometric Pb ions in perovskite layer and passivated the trap states [53].

One of most important limiting factors for commercialization in PSCs technology is the reproducibility. To see the effect of B-CQDs addition on the reproducibility results, 20 devices were fabricated with 5% vol. of B-CQDs. Statistical distributions data of the photovoltaic parameters ( Fig. 8) demonstrated that the average efficiency and FF for devices with B-CQDs additive were high. It could be due to the passivation effect and better coverage of perovskite layer.

In this study, B-CQDs was successfully synthesized by facile way, and it was utilized as additive to engineer the properties of MAPbI3 absorber layer in PSCs for improved performance. The morphological characteristics indicated that the surface defects on perovskite thin films and coverage can be tuned by B-CQDs addition. Optimization studies results of additive volume showed that the appropriate addition of B-CQDs (5% vol.) into perovskite solution resulted a strong effect to passivate the grain boundaries of perovskite crystals, improve the crystallinity, and promote the charge carrier transport property. Based on our approach, the champion PSC increased from initial PCE of 10.4% to PCE of 12.7%, thanks to improved optical and morphological properties, band structure, and better charge collection. Additionally, the reproducibility of the PSCs was also observed around 80%. The simple solution-based method proposed in this study afford further manipulation to fabricate perovskite-based photovoltaics with desired properties including stability, high-performance PCSs, low-cost fabrication.

Çisem Kırbıyık Kurukavak: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Tuğbahan Yılmaz: Writing - original draft, Writing -review & editing, Investigation. Ayşegül Toprak: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Alihan Büyükbekar : Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Mahmut Kuş: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation. Mustafa Ersöz: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank to financial support of the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TÜBİTAK) (PN:121F377), EngSurf-Twin project (GA: 952289), European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme , Selcuk University Scientific Research Council (PN: 19101003) and Turkish Academy of Sciences (TÜBA).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.166851.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient perovskite solar cells via improved carrier management

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Yoo, G. Seo, M.R. Chua, T.G. Park, Y. Lu, F. Rotermund, Y.-K. Kim, C.S. Moon, N.J. Jeon, J.-P. Correa-Baena, V. Bulović, S.S. Shin, M.G. Bawendi, J. Seo, Efficient perovskite solar cells via improved carrier management, Nature, 590 (2021) 587–593.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drop-casting to make efficient perovskite solar cells under high humidity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zuo, L. Ding, Drop-Casting to Make Efficient Perovskite Solar Cells under High Humidity, Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 60, (2021), 11242.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacial modification and defect passivation by the cross-linking interlayer for efficient and stable cuscn-based perovskite solar cells
